# Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy

This document establishes a structured method for establishing the safe working conditions necessary for operations in which H&P employees may encounter Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S).

## 1. PURPOSE

The main purpose of this document is to provide information and advice to prevent accidents due to the exposure of personnel to Hydrogen Sulphide (H2S) or to substances containing Hydrogen Sulphide as well as to describe the system implemented on rig site to control this risk.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 1. Scope and Application
blocks.source_heading_path: HYDROGEN SULPHIDE > 1. PURPOSE
blocks.ai_reasoning: HP Candidate 0 covers the same core purpose-level content: both are introductory H2S control statements aimed at preventing exposure-related harm and framing safe operating conditions. The main difference is applicability context, with KCAD scoped to Saudi Arabia rigs while HP is corporate-wide, and KCAD does not add concrete operational detail beyond that local framing.

1.1. Prevent all incidents associated with H2S.

blocks.text:
## 2. Definitions

2.1. **Air Supplying Respirator (ASR)** - a device that provides Grade D breathing air. There are two types of ASR: Supplied Air Respirator (SAR) and Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA).

2.2. **All Clear** – indication that all H2S exposures have been mitigated and that work is allowed to resume. This is typically communicated via horn blast or rig communications system. This can only be given by a qualified person in the proper operation of fixed gas detection equipment.

2.3. **Contingency Plan** – a written document that contains emergency response procedures which provide an organized plan of action for alerting and protecting the public within an area of exposure, prior to an intentional or accidental release of a potentially hazardous volume of H2S. This should be provided by the operator, O&G exploration, or production company.

2.4. **Escape Pack** - an emergency escape-breathing device providing 5, 10 or 15 minutes of breathing air for escape from toxic environments, even in atmospheres immediately dangerous to life and health (IDLH). This includes SCBA masks and escape hoods.

2.5. **Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health (IDLH)** - exposure to airborne contaminants that is likely to cause death or may cause immediate or delayed permanent adverse health effects that might prevent escape from such an environment. For H2S this concentration is 100 ppm

2.6. **Iron Sulfide** – a chemical compound consisting of iron and sulfur, commonly found inside piping, vessels and/or other equipment where H2S is or has been present, that will ignite and burn in the presence of oxygen in air.

2.7. **Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S)** - a gas formed in nature by the decomposition of organic material by bacteria. H2S is found in natural gas, oil, sewers, stagnant water, volcanic gases, sulfur springs and anywhere that organic materials may be broken down.

2.8. **Lower Explosive Limit** – The lowest concentration (percentage) of a gas or vapor in air capable of producing a flash of fire in presence of an ignition source (arc, flame, heat). This concentration for H2S is 4% or 40,000 ppm.

2.9. **Parts Per Million (ppm)** - a concentration by volume of one part of a gas (or vapor), or by weight of a liquid or solid, per million parts of air or liquid.

2.10. **Radius of Exposure (ROE)** – the distance from a release to where H2S concentration in the air will dilute to a specific concentration.

2.11. **Self-contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA)** – a respirator that has compressed air contained on the device used by rescue team (rated for a maximum of 30 minutes).

2.12. **Sulfur Dioxide (SO2)** - a toxic gas created when hydrogen sulfide is burned. It is heavier than air and forms sulfuric acid when combined with moisture (including perspiration).

2.13. **Supplied Air Respirator (SAR)** - a respirator that has compressed air from a stationary source is supplied through a high-pressure hose connected to a full-face respirator under positive pressure/pressure demand with an auxiliary self-contained bottle (rated for a minimum of five minutes).

blocks.text:
## 3. H2S Description and Hazards

Hydrogen sulfide is a colorless, flammable, extremely hazardous gas with a “rotten egg” smell at concentrations under 100 ppm. Some common names for the gas include sewer gas, stink damp, swamp gas and manure gas. It occurs naturally in crude petroleum, natural gas, and hot springs. In addition, hydrogen sulfide is produced by bacterial breakdown of organic materials and human and animal wastes (e.g., sewage).

Hydrogen sulfide is heavier than air and may travel along the ground. It collects in low-lying and enclosed, poorly ventilated areas such as pits, cellars, and reserve pits.

The primary route of exposure is inhalation, and the gas is rapidly absorbed by the lungs. Absorption through the skin is minimal. People can smell the “rotten egg” odor of hydrogen sulfide at low concentrations in air. However, with continuous low-level exposure, or at high concentrations, a person loses his/her ability to smell the gas even though it is still present. This can happen very rapidly and at high concentrations (over 100 ppm), the ability to smell the gas can be lost instantaneously.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 3. H2S Description and Hazards

blocks.text:
### 5.3. HEALTH EFFECTS

If H2S is in sufficient concentration, it is a highly toxic gas, the toxic effects of exposure to H2S can be very rapid and death can occur very quickly.

The major hazard of H2S is its ability to cause sudden death due to accidental exposure.

The H2S smells of rotten eggs and can be detected at concentrations as low as 0.02 ppm, but for relatively low concentrations (100 ppm) the sense of smell is lost 2-15 minutes of exposure, consequently at the higher concentration (the most dangerous) H2S is not detectable by smell, and reliance must be placed on detection equipment for alert (fixed and portable H2S detection equipment).

blocks.source_heading_path: HYDROGEN SULPHIDE > 5. PROCEDURE > 5.3. HEALTH EFFECTS

blocks.ai_reasoning: HP candidate 0 covers the same informational hazard context: H2S odor, loss of smell at hazardous concentrations, and the resulting unreliability of smell as a warning sign. KCAD is best treated as a Variant because it adds concrete numeric and equipment detail within a Saudi Arabia context rather than merely restating HP. HP candidate 2 is complementary because it converts the same hazard logic into a mandatory rule not to rely on smell.

blocks.text:
Hydrogen Sulphide is denser than air and will accumulate in low-lying areas as well as cellars, tanks, pits, etc., and can poison somebody or explode many days after the gas escaped.

As Hydrogen Sulphide is soluble in both water and hydrocarbons, it may be encountered in oil and gas wells, water wells, stagnant water, sewers, etc., and in general in confined spaces.

Hydrogen Sulphide, in combination with water, forms a weak acid that will attack metals in the same manner as carbon dioxide or oxygen. Hydrogen Sulphide embitters steel and other materials.

## 3. H2S Description and Hazards

Hydrogen sulfide is a colorless, flammable, extremely hazardous gas with a “rotten egg” smell at concentrations under 100 ppm. Some common names for the gas include sewer gas, stink damp, swamp gas and manure gas. It occurs naturally in crude petroleum, natural gas, and hot springs. In addition, hydrogen sulfide is produced by bacterial breakdown of organic materials and human and animal wastes (e.g., sewage).

Hydrogen sulfide is heavier than air and may travel along the ground. It collects in low-lying and enclosed, poorly ventilated areas such as pits, cellars, and reserve pits.

The primary route of exposure is inhalation, and the gas is rapidly absorbed by the lungs. Absorption through the skin is minimal. People can smell the “rotten egg” odor of hydrogen sulfide at low concentrations in air. However, with continuous low-level exposure, or at high concentrations, a person loses his/her ability to smell the gas even though it is still present. This can happen very rapidly and at high concentrations (over 100 ppm), the ability to smell the gas can be lost instantaneously.

blocks.text: Therefore, employees MUST NOT rely on their sense of smell to indicate the continuing presence of hydrogen sulfide or to warn of hazardous concentrations.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 3. H2S Description and Hazards

blocks.text:
In addition, hydrogen sulfide is a highly flammable gas and gas/air mixtures can be explosive. It may travel to sources of ignition and flash back. If ignited, the gas burns to produce toxic vapors and gases, such as sulfur dioxide.

Hydrogen sulfide is both an irritant and a chemical asphyxiate with effects on both oxygen utilization and the central nervous system. Its health effects can vary depending on the level and duration of exposure. Repeated exposure can result in health effects occurring at levels that were previously tolerated without any effect.

Low concentrations irritate the eyes, nose, throat, and respiratory system (e.g., burning/ tearing of eyes, cough, shortness of breath). Asthmatics may experience breathing difficulties. The effects can be delayed for several hours, or sometimes several days, when working in low-level concentrations. Repeated or prolonged exposures may cause eye inflammation, headache, fatigue, irritability, insomnia, digestive disturbances, and weight loss. Moderate concentrations can cause more severe eye and respiratory irritation (including coughing, difficulty breathing, and accumulation of fluid in the lungs), headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, staggering and excitability.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 3. H2S Description and Hazards

blocks.text:
## 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment

### 4.1. H2S Personal Monitor Sounds

When an H2S Personal Monitor sounds, personnel will:

4.1.1. Alert other crew members and the driller who will sound the muster/evacuation alarm.

4.1.2. H&P personnel will secure well, evacuate rig and monitor for H2S in accordance with the agreed upon plan between H&P and the Operator, based upon well conditions.

4.1.3. Don an escape pack (if one is near)

4.1.4. Move cross wind or up wind to the designated muster area and await further instructions.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.1. H2S Personal Monitor Sounds

blocks.text:
### 4.2. Fixed H2S Monitor detects H2S

When a fixed H2S Monitor detects H2S, the light will flash and the siren will sound indicating a release of H2S. Personnel will:

4.2.1. Alert other crew members and the driller who will sound the muster/evacuation alarm.

4.2.2. H&P personnel will secure well, evacuate rig and monitor for H2S in accordance with the agreed upon plan between H&P and the Operator, based upon well conditions.

4.2.3. Don an escape pack (if one is near).

4.2.4. Move cross wind or up wind to the designated muster area and await further instructions.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.2. Fixed H2S Monitor detects H2S

blocks.text:
### 4.3. Muster Area

Upon arriving at the Muster Area, a head count will be conducted to account for everyone on location.

4.3.1. If someone is missing a 2-man designated rescue team will mask up in Self Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBAs) to search and if possible, to retrieve the missing person. Escape packs will not be used for rescue operations.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.3. Muster Area

blocks.text: 4.3.2. Only H&P employees that completed a respiratory medical history reviewed with a licensed healthcare professional, pulmonary function testing and a qualitative or quantitative respirator fit testing to the SCBA models present and received proper SCBA training may be part of a designated search and rescue team.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.3. Muster Area

blocks.text: 4.3.3. Operations will cease until all personnel on site are accounted for.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.3. Muster Area

blocks.text:
### 4.4. Cleaning Alarms

4.4.1. H&P personnel will not resume normal operations until a trained H2S technician wearing proper supplied air equipment and carrying a functional/calibrated H2S detector has determined the cause of the alarm.

a. H&P personnel are allowed to return to work utilizing SARs in atmospheres with H2S levels between 10 ppm and 1,000 ppm.

b. H&P personnel are allowed to return to work without utilizing SARs in atmospheres with H2S below 10 ppm.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.4. Cleaning Alarms

blocks.text: 4.4.2. When the Operator’s personnel are formally trained as H2S technicians for the type of fixed gas detection system present they may enter the affected area when under mask to test for H2S and declare “all clear”.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.4. Cleaning Alarms

blocks.text: 4.4.3. Normal operations will then resume.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.4. Cleaning Alarms

blocks.text:
4.4.4. Operator’s personnel may determine the cause of the alarm when formally trained as a H2S technician.

4.4.5. H&P personnel are not formally trained on fixed gas detection equipment and are not authorized to clear alarms.

4.4.6. H&P employees that have been respirator fit tested to the SCBA model present and received proper SCBA training may accompany third party H2S and/or Operator personnel utilizing the buddy system when entering possible contaminated areas.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 4. Response Procedure and Working in an H2S Environment > 4.4. Cleaning Alarms

blocks.text:
### 4.5. Working in High H2S Environment (10 < 1000 PPM H2S)

4.5.1. When the area is deemed to be an H2S environment the Operator will call out a 3rd party H2S equipment provider with a work unit trailer.

The Safety Officer shall ensure that all persons likely to work in possible H2S environment are fully aware and instructed in the use of safety equipment.

He shall further verify that all safety equipment is fully maintained, inspected and available for use as required by contractual requirements.

### 6.2. WORK / ESCAPE BREATHING APPARATUS WITH FULL-FACE MASK

The work / escape breathing apparatus provides respiratory protection for protracted period in toxic or oxygen deficient atmosphere with the capability for the wearer to leave such atmospheres at any time without losing respiratory protection.

The apparatus is combination of a short duration (rated for 10 minutes or longer) escape type self-contained breathing apparatus and a pressure demand type air-line respirator. In normal use, the apparatus is connected to air cascade system. The pressure demand air-line respirator provides respiratory protection until the user disconnects from the air supply using the self-contained breathing apparatus to return to fresh air.

The maintenance, inspection and storage requirements for the Work / Escape Sets are like SCBA.

### 6.1. SELF-CONTAINED BREATHING APPARATUS (SCBA) WITH FULL-FACE MASK

A positive-pressure system is recommended in presence of H2S into atmosphere. To prevent a negative pressure being created in the mask during inhalation, with ingress of toxic gases via the face seal, only positive-pressure and not demand-type units should be used. Positive-pressure units incorporate a pressure reducer together with a mechanically operated demand regulator and a pre-set expiration valve. This design prevents negative pressures occurring within the face mask during inhalation.

### 4.3. TOOLPUSHER

The Toolpusher is responsible for the implementation of this procedure on the rig site.

- Ensuring that all persons are informed about dangers associated with H2S occurrence.
- Promoting measures that eliminate or reduce H2S risk wherever practicable.
- Ensuring that all persons potentially exposed to H2S are informed about the hazards and risks associated with H2S emergency.
- Ensuring adequate resources availability for the provision of proper Respiratory Protective Equipment, as well as the concerned accredited training for all personnel potentially exposed to H2S.
- Ensuring that the emergency planning associated to the H2S release event is in place, concerned drills conducted, the relevant HSE Risk assessment performed, and the correct Work Permit system is enforced, where necessary, to the activities where presence of H2S is possible.

## 5. Respirators, Air Quality, and Use

5.1. Respirators using atmosphere-supplying respirators (supplied-air and SCBA) shall provide users with high purity breathing gases. Air Purifying respirators (half mask or cartridge) cannot be used in a H2S environment.

5.2. Compressed air, compressed oxygen, liquid air, and liquid oxygen used for respirators will meet or exceed the following specifications:

5.2.1. Compressed breathing air shall meet at least the requirements for Type 1-Grade D breathing air described in ANSI/Compressed Gas Association Commodity Specification for Air, G-7.1-1989, to include:

5.2.2. Oxygen content (v/v) of 19.5-23.5%.

5.2.3. Hydrocarbon (condensed) content of 5 milligrams per cubic meter of air or less.

5.2.4. Carbon monoxide (CO) content of 10 or less; and

5.2.5. Carbon dioxide content of 1,000 ppm or less; and

5.2.6. Lack of noticeable odor.

5.3. Compressed oxygen must not be used in atmosphere-supplying respirators that have previously used compressed air.

5.4. Cylinders used to supply breathing air to respirators must meet the following requirements:

5.4.1. Cylinders are tested and maintained as prescribed in the Shipping Container Specification Regulations of the Department of Transportation (49 CFR part 173, 178).

5.4.2. Cylinders of purchased breathing air have a certificate of analysis from the supplier that the breathing air meets the requirements for Type 1-Grade D breathing air; and

5.4.3. The moisture content in the cylinder does not exceed a dew point of –50 deg F (-45.6 deg C) at 1 atmosphere pressure.

5.5. Compressors used to supply breathing air to respirators must be constructed and situated to:

5.5.1. Prevent entry of contaminated air into the air-supply system.

5.5.2. Minimize moisture content so that the dew point at 1 atmosphere pressure is 10 degrees F (5.56 deg C) below the ambient temperature.

5.5.3. Have suitable in-line air-purifying sorbent beds and filters to further ensure breathing air quality.

5.5.4. Sorbent bed and filters shall be maintained and replaced or refurbished periodically following the manufacturer’s instructions.

5.5.5. Have a tag containing the most recent change date and the signature of the person authorized by the employer to perform the change. The tag shall be maintained at the compressor.

5.6. For compressors that are not oil-lubricated, carbon monoxide levels in the breathing air must not exceed 10 ppm.

5.7. For oil-lubricated compressors, a high-temperature or carbon monoxide alarm, or both, must be used to monitor carbon monoxide levels. If only high-temperature alarms are used, the air supply shall be monitored at intervals sufficient to prevent carbon monoxide in the breathing air from exceeding 10 ppm.

5.8. Breathing air couplings must not be compatible with outlets for non-respirable work-site air or other gas systems. No asphyxiating substance shall be introduced into breathing airlines.

5.9. Breathing gas containers must be marked in accordance with the NIOSH respirator certification standard.

blocks.text:
## 6. Training

Employees whose job requirements may put them in atmospheric environments with potential concentrations of H2S more than 10 ppm in the work-area atmosphere shall have training before reporting to their first assignment, as well as annual training in subjects including, but not limited to, the items below. All training will be documented in each employee’s personal training record which will be maintained in the Company’s training database.

6.1. The hazards, characteristics, properties, and sources of H2S (SDS)

6.2. Danger in relying on sense of smell. Individuals may not be able to smell an odor after an extended period of exposure to it, because they become accustomed to the odor.

6.3. Fixed, intrinsically safe, UL-listed H2S gas detection monitors will be rigged up. Recommended locations include at the rig floor, bell nipple, cellar, trip tanks, shale shakers, mud tanks and mud pump room.

6.4. Proper use of H2S detection methods and monitoring equipment and availability of monitoring information

6.5. Effects of H2S on equipment (metal fatigue)

6.6. Symptoms of overexposure to H2S

6.6.1. Emergency medical procedures as outlined in the facility contingency plan, including first aid for H2S overexposures.

6.6.2. Proper use of and maintenance of respiratory and eye protection equipment.

6.7. Demonstrate proficiency with three types of supplied air breathing apparatus available at the rig site, the escape pack and a 30-minute Self Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA). As well as proficiency with the cascade SAR system, which may be available.

6.8. Safe work procedures and precautions to protect employees from H2S.

6.8.1. Emergency response and rescue procedures should rescue become necessary.

6.8.2. Circumstances when a standby person in supplied air or SCBA is required.

6.8.3. Wind direction awareness and safe routes of egress.

6.8.4. Job hazards

6.8.5. Confined and enclosed space entry procedures (where applicable)

6.8.6. Applicable alarm signals the right to access exposure monitoring results, SDSs, chemical inventory and medical records. Training shall be on an on-going basis and be conducted by qualified H&P personnel. Periodic drills on the use of the respirators and rescue of workers should be included as part of the training program.

blocks.text:
### 7.2. H2S TRAINING AND DRILLS

All persons who may be exposed to H2S should receive special instructions on the health hazards and the procedures and precautionary measures intended for their protection.

The following subjects should be included in the above training:

- Health hazards.
- Emergency and rescue procedures.
- Respiratory protective equipment.
- First aid for victims of H2S exposure.

Operating personnel should be specifically trained in rescue techniques to enable recovery of persons who may have been overcome by exposure to H2S.

This training should include:

- Rapid donning of SCBA with full-face mask (All personnel on the rig site must be able to don the breathing apparatus within 45 sec.).
- Methods of lifting unconscious persons while wearing SCBA.
- Application of mouth-to-mouth respiration.
- Operation of a mechanical resuscitator.

### 4.5. ALL PERSONNEL

All personnel prior arrival on rig site shall undergo the accredited H2S Training and comply with all safety precaution measures foreseen for the rig site.

They are responsible to attend the Rig HSE Induction conducted by the Safety Officer, upon their first arrival on the rig site.

They shall be familiar with safety information and emergency instructions given / displayed on site as well as with personal protective equipment layout, use and storage.

blocks.text:
## 7. Equipment Location and Precautionary Measures

A minimum of two Safe Briefing Areas will be established, not less than 150 feet, or as practical from the wellhead and in such a location that at least one area will be always up wind of the well. Upon recognition of an emergency, all personnel must assemble at the designated, up wind briefing area, for muster and instructions.

7.1. A minimum of (5) H2S detectors will be installed throughout rig – One at the rig floor, one on rotary beam near bell nipple (if feasible with equipment being provided), one in the cellar, one in active pit, and one on the shale shakers – to provide continuous H2S detection monitoring.

blocks.text:
The H₂S monitoring system shall include visible and audible alarms that can be heard at all places on the drilling location and shall be triggered at a level of 10 ppm of H₂S. A minimum of three H₂S sensors shall be installed as follows:

- one in shaker area,
- one on the drill floor
- one in the sub structure.

## 1.2 Procedure for Calibration and Calibration Check:

**Client Requirement:**

*Permanent atmospheric H₂S & HC monitoring systems shall be calibrated and tested prior to spudding or re-entering a well. Routine testing and calibration check shall be performed on a bi-weekly (every two weeks) basis.*

*In order to ensure that the system functions to its expected reliability, it shall be serviced and calibrated by approved agencies on an annual basis, in line with the manufacturer’s Instruction manual.*

##### 1.2.1.2 System Calibration:

a. Make sure the sensor is seeing clean air  
b. Place H₂S ampoule – 50 % of full scale (10 ppm) in to a breaker bottle; do not place the breaker bottle over the sensor until display shows flashing AC.  
c. Enter the Calibration mode by pressing and holding the Mode/Select switch until the CAL LED turns on steady.  
d. When the CAL LED is on steady, release the Mode/Select switch and wait for an ‘AC’ indication on the display. The unit is now in the Calibration Mode.  

**If the Portable H₂S Calibrator is used instead of ampoules, do as follows:**

1. Before placing the unit in the CAL mode, make sure the gas concentration of the portable purge calibrator equals 50% of full scale for the sensor being calibrated and prepare the Portable Flow Calibrator. Place the ON/OFF knob on the regulator in the ON position, then securely fasten the regulator to the Portable Flow cylinder.
2. Place the unit in the CAL mode, as per ‘c’ above.
3. Do not place the cup attached to the Portable Flow cylinder over the sensor until the display shows AC, then follow the rest of the procedure.

- Break the ampoule (10 ppm) and watch the display change from “AC” to “CP” as the sensor see gas.
- If the display does not change from “AC” to “CP” after twelve minutes, the monitor will return to normal operation. Display not changing means sensor is not seeing gas. Check wiring and gas source for problem. If still does not change to “CP”, change sensor.
- Wait for the display to change from “CP” to “CC” when the calibration is complete (about 2 minutes). If the display does not change from “CP” to “CC” after twelve minutes, the monitor will indicate a Fault condition. Attempt another calibration.
- Remove the gas and watch the display return to normal operation, “0” when the new calibration values have been stored in the EEPROM.
- If the unit cannot store the new calibration values in the EEPROM, the unit will display an F7 fault code (EEPROM verification failure). If any F7 calibration fault occurs, it will be necessary to replace the Model 2602A. If the Model 2602A fails to calibrate, the unit will use the previously stored calibrated values.

##### 1.2.2.2 System Calibration:

a. Place an ampoule corresponding to full scale H2S concentration ( 20 ppm ) in the field calibrator which has been purged with clean air, and gas the sensor.  
b. Allow approx. 5 minutes for the reading to stabilise. Set the meter to read full scale using the Upper potentiometer. Remove the field calibrator and allow the sensor to be permeated with clean air until the reading stabilises at a zero value.  
c. Remove all glass fragments from the field calibrator and insert an ampoule corresponding to ± 20% (5ppm) of full scale concentration. Again gas the sensor and allow approximately 5 minutes for the reading to stabilise. Set the meter to read 5 ppm. using the Low potentiometer and remove the field calibrator. Allow the reading to stabilise at zero.  
d. Calibration check: Remove all glass fragments from the field calibrator and insert an ampoule corresponding to ± 50% ( 10 ppm ) of full scale of the sensor in the field calibrator. Again gas the sensor and allow approximately 5 minutes for the reading to stabilise. You should read 10 ppm ± 2 ppm. If not, repeat steps a to c.  
e. Using the calibration tool, slide the calibration door to the right. The ‘Ready’ indicator should now be illuminated constantly and the ‘Fault’ indicator should be extinguished.

##### 1.2.1.1 Calibration Check:

- Place an H₂S ampoule corresponding to 50 % (10 ppm) of full scale of the sensor in to a breaker bottle; do not place the breaker bottle over the sensor until display shows a flashing Zero.
- *Enter the Calibration Check mode by pressing and holding the Mode/Select switch until the CAL LED begins to flash (about 10 seconds). When the CAL LED begins to flash, release the Mode/Select switch. The unit is now in the Calibration check Mode.
- The A1 & A2 alarms are disabled during the calibration check mode.
- When the Mode/Select switch is released, the display will indicate a flashing 0 .

**If the Portable Flow Calibrator is used instead of ampoules, do as follows:**

1. Before placing the unit in the CAL check mode, make sure the Portable Purge Calibrator contains a gas concentration that does not exceed full scale for the sensor and prepare the Portable Flow Calibrator. Place the ON/OFF knob on the regulator in the ON position, then securely fasten the regulator to the Portable Flow cylinder.
2. Place the unit in the CAL Check Mode as per the Instructions* in para-2 above.
3. Do not place the cup attached to the Portable Flow cylinder over the sensor until the display shows a flashing Zero, then follow the procedure below:

- Apply the test gas to the sensor (break the ampoule) and wait for a few seconds. The display will begin to go upscale as the sensor sees the gas. If the display does not change after 12 minutes, the unit will return to the normal operating mode. If the sensor continues to see the calibration check gas for 12minutes, an F9 fault will be displayed, which states that the calibration check period has exceeded.
- If the sensor does see the gas, the read-out on the display will be flashing
- The reading will stabilise after a minute or two.
- Compare the reading with the gas concentration applied and decide if it is necessary to calibrate the sensor. (You must read 10 ppm ± 2 ppm)
- If the reading is acceptable, remove the gas & allow sensor to see clean air.
- *If the system is to be recalibrated, proceed with the following steps:*

1. If the applied gas concentration is 50 % of full scale, place the unit in the calibration mode by pressing the Mode/Select switch or
2. If the applied gas concentration is not 50 % of full scale, remove the gas for at least 5 minutes, then follow the calibration procedure given below:

##### 1.2.3.1 System Calibration:

a. Place magnet over GM logo. Display will change to “--” indicating that the magnet is correctly positioned. Leave the magnet in place while display changes to “AC” (Auto Calibration).  
b. Put a 5 ppm ampoule in the field calibrator and place the calibrator over the sensor. Ensure that it is a snug fit. Gas the sensor. The display will change to “CP” (Calibration in Progress) when the instrument responds to the rising gas concentration. Wait until the display reads “LC” (Low Complete).  
c. Remove the field calibrator from the sensor and discard all glass fragments.  
d. Put a 20 ppm ampoule in the field calibrator and place the calibrator over the sensor. Ensure that it fits snugly. Gas the sensor. The display will change to “CP” (Calibration in Progress).Wait until the display reads“CC” (Calibration Complete).  
e. Calibration Check: Position the magnet over the GM logo. The symbol “--” will be displayed for three seconds and then will begin to flash. Remove the magnet and the display will now flash with the gas concentration at the sensor. Remove all glass fragments from the field calibrator and insert an ampoule corresponding to 50% (10ppm) of full scale of the sensor, in the field calibrator. Again gas the sensor and observe the display. You must read 10 ppm ± 2 ppm. If not, proceed again to steps a to d.  
f. Remove the field calibrator from the sensor and discard all glass fragments. The display will continue to flash until the gas has been removed and the level at the sensor drops below 1 ppm.

7.5. All monitors and alarms will be bump tested per manufacturer’s guidelines.

7.7.1. On top of the Driller’s Cabin / Top doghouse.

blocks.text: An adequate number of escape packs should be present for the number of persons that are commonly in that area.

blocks.text: 7.9. When working in areas that have H2S potential, an escape pack will be kept at the landing zone for the emergency descent device. The only time an escape pack may be taken to the board, as a potential dropped object exposure, is when analysis indicates that H2S is present in the drilling mud at 10ppm or greater. Any time an escape pack is taken to the board, the escape pack must be tethered to the employee while it is being transported, to eliminate a potential dropped object exposure. Once the employee reaches the board with an escape pack, the escape pack must be secured to a fixed object on the derrick board.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 7. Equipment Location and Precautionary Measures

blocks.text: 7.10. All H&P employees in potential H2S environments (those wells classified as “H2S”) will be clean shaven (i.e. no stubble). Employees in potential H2S environments may NOT wear beards or facial hair that may prevent proper sealing of the breathing apparatus / respirator. They may wear mustaches that are trimmed clear of the sealing area but are otherwise required to be clean shaven. Employees working in environments in which H2S is not usually present, in which escape hoods are used in lieu of masks are exempted from this requirement as it pertains to the H2S standard. However, all employees that are enrolled in a Respiratory Protection Program that requires the use of respirators, will remain clean shaven.
blocks.heading_path: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S) Policy > 7. Equipment Location and Precautionary Measures

blocks.text: To protect the workforce, industry best practice, legislative advice, occupational safety, and health professional bodies as well as equipment manufacturers prescribe that wearer of tight-fitting face-piece RPE be fit tested to ensure a tight seal around the mask, which is essential in ensuring the life of the wearer in such toxic environments. To achieve a seal, the wearer must be clean shaven. The clean-shaven policy is applicable for the SCBA use.

However, all employees that are enrolled in a Respiratory Protection Program that requires the use of respirators, will remain clean shaven.

## 8. Symptoms and Effects of H2S at Different Levels | Concentration (ppm) | Symptoms/Effects | |---|---| | 0.00011-0.00033 | Typical background concentrations | | 0.01-1.5 | Odor threshold (when rotten egg smell is first noticeable to some). Odor becomes more offensive at 3-5 ppm.
Above 30 ppm, odor described as sweet or sickeningly sweet. | | 10 | Highest allowable H2S atmosphere for H&P employees to perform work in without SARs.
Any time H2S levels exceed 10 ppm, SCBAs or Cascade Systems must be utilized. | | 20 | Possible fatigue, loss of appetite, headache, irritability, poor memory, dizziness. | | 50-100 | Slight conjunctivitis ("gas eye") and respiratory tract irritation after 1 hour.
May cause digestive upset and loss of appetite. | | 100 | Coughing, eye irritation, loss of smell after 2-15 minutes (olfactory fatigue). Altered breathing, drowsiness after 15-30 minutes. Throat irritation. Gradual increase in severity of symptoms over several hours. Death may occur after 48 hours. | | 100-150 | Loss of smell (olfactory fatigue or paralysis).
100 ppm Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health (IDLH) | | 200-300 | Marked conjunctivitis and respiratory tract irritation after 1 hour.
Pulmonary edema may occur from prolonged exposure. | | 500-700 | Staggering, collapse in 5 minutes. Nervous system effects prevail over eyes or respiratory. Death after 30-60 minutes. | | 700-1000 | Rapid unconsciousness, "knockdown" or immediate collapse within 1 to 2 breaths, breathing stops, death within minutes. | | 1,000 | All operations cease, even work with employees utilizing supplied air | | 1000-2000 | Possible instant death | | 43,000 - 460,000 | H2S becomes Flammable |

### 5.6. H2S ALERT LEVELS

During drilling in H2S zones, the rig will conduct operations based on the following classifications of H2S Risk.

| Alert level | H2S Concentration (H2S) | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| Level 0 | (H2S) = 0ppm | Normal Operations |
| Level 1 | 0ppm <(H2S) <10 ppm | Normal Operations, but increasing alertness, preparing for next level. Restricted access to affected area. |
| Level2 | 10ppm <(H2S) <100 ppm (but not on the rig floor) | Restricted access and breathing apparatus required in affected areas (H2S)>10ppm. Non-essential personnel removed from rig package. Immediate action taken to reduce mud H2S levels. Low Alarm at 10 ppm, high alarm at 20 ppm. |
| Level3 | 10ppm <(H2S) <100 ppm (on the rig floor) | Approaching an Emergency Situation. Low Alarm at 10 ppm, High Alarm at 20 ppm. |
| H2S Emergency | (H2S)>100ppm | Action taken to reduce H2S concentrations have failed, and an emergency has been reached. |

H2S emergency response shall be organised with reference to Rig Emergency Response Plan (specific for each rig).

### 1.2 SAFETY INFORMATION

**WARNING:** The Driller must ensure that the Floor men are outside the area of the tongs before pulling on the tongs, if an abnormal amount of torque is to be applied during make up or break out.

**CAUTION:** Snub lines for the rig tongs must be inspected and to be in good working Condition.

**CAUTION** Hanging lines for the rig tongs must be inspected and to be in good working Condition.

**CAUTION:** The Floor man must be instructed on proper placement of hands while operating the rig tongs.

**CAUTION:** Never stand between engaged rotary tongs.

**CAUTION:** Never use rotary tongs without stop lines attached.

### 1.3 STANDARD OPERATIONAL CONSIDERATIONS (DO’S AND DON’TS)

(1) Always use two sets of rig tongs of the proper size to break out or make up connections.

(2) The Floor men must lubricate rig tongs at regular intervals, and always before and after each trip.

(3) The slack in the rig tong safety line must be sufficient for the tong to obtain full benefit from the pull of the cathead, but short enough to prevent half rotation of the rig tong. The length of the stop lines are pre-defined and are different for each rig, depending on the geometry of the drill floor.

(4) Stop lines must not be misused for other purposes.

### 1.4 INSTRUCTIONS

#### Breaking Out Connections with the Rig Tongs

(1) The Floor men remove the safety latch springs and the tong pins and change the tong jaws to match the size of the pipe or tubular to be broken out.

(2) The Driller ensures that the safety latch springs are replaced on the tong pins.

(3) The Floor men set the slips, or where applicable, the Driller sets the automatic slips.

(4) The Floor men engage the rig tongs on the drill pipe or tubular tool joint. (The breakout tong is placed on the top tool joint and the makeup tong is placed on the bottom tool joint).

#### Making Up Connections with the Rig Tongs

(1) The floor men remove the safety latch springs and the tong pins and change the tong jaws to match the size of the pipe or tubular to be made up.

(2) The Driller ensures that the safety latch springs are replaced on the tong pins.

(3) The floor men set the slips and the Driller picks up a joint or a stand of pipe, or where applicable, the Driller sets the automatic slips.

(4) The floor men check the thread and seal area and apply pipe dope to the box or pin end of the connection thoroughly.

(5) The floor man guides the pin end of the joint into the box of the joint in the rotary table, while the Driller lowers the pipe.

(6) The floor men rotate the pipe into the box utilising the air spinner or where applicable, the Driller utilises the top-drive.

(7) The Floor men engage the rig tongs onto the drill pipe or tubular tool joint (The makeup tong is placed on the top tool joint and the breakout tong is placed on the bottom tool joint). The Floor men make the rig tongs bite by pulling on the tong jaws and pushing on the tong arm.
